articles.title: Reggie Fils-Aimé says Amazon once asked Nintendo to break the law
articles.description: Way back in the DS days, Nintendo decided to stop selling to Amazon. During a recent lecture at NYU, former Nintendo of America President Reggie Fils-Aimé said it was because Amazon was seeking preferential treatment that would have hurt its relationship with other retailers, and potentially br
articles.content: Way back in the DS days, Nintendo decided to stop selling to Amazon. During a recent lecture at NYU , former Nintendo of America President Reggie Fils-Aimé said it was because Amazon was seeking preferential treatment that would have hurt its relationship with other retailers, and potentially broken the law . The two sides have since made amends , and you can buy a Switch 2 through Amazon. But for a long time, Nintendo consoles had been largely unavailable on the site. In the 2000s, Amazo
articles.author: Terrence O’Brien

It was a surprise success - but what's even more surprising is the app's origin story: it all started with a cadre of friendly neighborhood squirrels and their favorite caretaker. articles.title: Serverless Workflow Decomposition: When a Step Function Becomes a Monolith
articles.description: There is a point in many serverless platforms where a Step Functions workflow that once felt elegant starts to feel like a mini application platform of its own. I have seen this happen in teams that are doing many things correctly: they standardized orchestration, they improved visibility, and they
articles.content: There is a point in many serverless platforms where a Step Functions workflow that once felt elegant starts to feel like a mini application platform of its own. I have seen this happen in teams that are doing many things correctly: they standardized orchestration, they improved visibility, and they moved fragile glue logic out of Lambdas. Then six months later, the workflow has 100+ states, a maze of Choice branches, deeply nested payload transformations, and a deployment blast radius that makes
articles.author: Renaldi

articles.title: Mathematically Prohibiting 'Cheating' in On-Chain RPS: A Midnight ZK dApp Case Study
articles.description: Introduction Implementing Rock-Paper-Scissors (RPS) on-chain is surprisingly tricky. The moment you choose "Rock" and send a transaction, your opponent can read your move from the public ledger. The game is over before it even starts. I tried implementing a commit-reveal pattern manually, but managi
articles.content: Introduction Implementing Rock-Paper-Scissors (RPS) on-chain is surprisingly tricky. The moment you choose "Rock" and send a transaction, your opponent can read your move from the public ledger. The game is over before it even starts. I tried implementing a commit-reveal pattern manually, but managing salts, preventing front-running, and ensuring fair judging logic... it quickly became a rabbit hole. That's when I turned to Midnight . I built a full-stack RPS dApp where "cheating" (looking at th
articles.author: Haruki Kondo

articles.title: Cognitive Infrastructure: the missing layer between AI and real impact
articles.description: Artificial Intelligence is everywhere. But meaningful results are not. We reached a point where access is no longer the problem. Tools are abundant. Content is abundant. “AI-powered” workflows are everywhere. Even so, most professionals are still thinking and operating at the same level as before. S
articles.content: Artificial Intelligence is everywhere. But meaningful results are not. We reached a point where access is no longer the problem. Tools are abundant. Content is abundant. “AI-powered” workflows are everywhere. Even so, most professionals are still thinking and operating at the same level as before. Something is off. The issue is not the technology itself. It is how it is being used. Right now, most people interact with AI as if it were a smarter search engine. You ask something, you get an answer
articles.author: AllRox

articles.title: These reusable digital Polaroids are a clever way to cover a fridge in memories
articles.description: I love when my friends have their fridges covered in Polaroids. It’s often a visual crash course of what’s been going on in their lives chronicling recent adventures, get-togethers, and other fun moments. But I don’t love the idea of schlepping around a clunky instant camera or paying a premium for
articles.content: You can update this photo as often as you want. I love when my friends have their fridges covered in Polaroids. It’s often a visual crash course of what’s been going on in their lives chronicling recent adventures, get-togethers, and other fun moments. But I don’t love the idea of schlepping around a clunky instant camera or paying a premium for instant film with hit-or-miss results. That’s why I’m infatuated with these magnetic digital Polaroids from a small company called VidaBay . They look l
articles.author: Andrew Liszewski

articles.title: Research roundup: 6 cool science stories we almost missed
articles.description: Crushing soda cans for science, why dolphins swim so fast, how urine helps mushrooms communicate, and more
articles.content: It’s a regrettable reality that there is never enough time to cover all the interesting scientific stories we come across. So every month, we highlight a handful of the best stories that nearly slipped through the cracks. April’s list includes tracking Roman ship repairs, the discovery that mushrooms can detect human urine, crushing soda cans for science, and the physics of why dolphins can swim so fast. Physics of why dolphins swim so fast Dolphins are very good swimmers but the exact mechanism
articles.author: Jennifer Ouellette

articles.title: Infrasound waves stop kitchen fires, but can they replace sprinklers?
articles.description: Acoustic fire suppression goes commercial.
articles.content: In a makeshift demonstration kitchen in Concord, California, cooking oil splatters in and around a frying pan, which catches fire on an unattended gas stove. Within moments, a smoke detector wails. But in this demonstration, something less common happens: An AI-driven sensor activates and wall emitters blast infrasound waves toward the source of the fire in an attempt to put it out. The science of acoustic fire suppression, which has long been known and documented in scientific literature and th
articles.author: Cyrus Farivar
